questionText: You are replacing a fryer element. The kitchen manager says 'just flip the breaker, we need it back fast.' The correct action is:

choices.text: Flip the breaker and work quickly
Apply your lock and tag to the breaker, verify dead with live-dead-live, then work
Have the manager stand by the breaker while you work
Work the element live since it is only 208V

explanation: LOTO is not negotiable regardless of schedule pressure. Your lock, your tag, and live-dead-live meter verification protect you from re-energization — a manager standing guard does not.

questionText: A restaurant replaced their griddle with a used one from another location. It heats poorly. The data plate reads 240V 3Ø; your meter reads 208V phase-to-phase at the terminal block. Element wattage is reduced to approximately:

choices.text: 95% of rating
87% of rating
75% of rating
50% of rating

explanation: Power scales with voltage squared: (208/240)² ≈ 0.75. The used 240V griddle delivers about three-quarters of its rated output on this 208V service — the equipment is mismatched to the supply, not broken.

questionText: With power locked out and one lead lifted, a 208V 3000W steamer element measures 14.4 ohms. This element is:

choices.text: Open and must be replaced
Within expected range and likely healthy
Shorted to its sheath
Drawing double its rated current

explanation: Expected R = V²/P = 208² ÷ 3000 ≈ 14.4 ohms — exactly what was measured. Predicting element resistance turns the ohmmeter into a pass/fail tester.

questionText: An oven's contactor coil measures 0V across its terminals while the thermostat calls for heat. The next diagnostic move is:

choices.text: Replace the contactor
Replace the elements
Trace the control chain upstream (switches, thermostat, high-limit, door switch) for the open point
Increase the supply voltage

explanation: No coil voltage means the control circuit is interrupted upstream. The schematic's control chain — every switch and safety between line and coil — is searched for the open; the contactor itself is only condemned when voltage is present and it fails to pull in.

questionText: A fryer's oil reaches 410°F with the thermostat set at 350°F before the high-limit trips. You find the thermostat contacts welded closed. Beyond replacing the thermostat, you must:

choices.text: Nothing — the new thermostat solves it
Verify the high-limit still trips at its rated temperature and functions correctly, since it just served as the only line of defense
Raise the high-limit setpoint for more margin
Install a lower-wattage element

explanation: The high-limit just did its job as the last defense against an oil fire. After any runaway event, verify the safety device that caught it still performs — and never adjust or bypass it.

questionText: A walk-in cooler: box at 50°F, compressor running continuously, suction pressure low, superheat high, subcooling normal, condenser clean. The most likely fault is:

choices.text: Refrigerant undercharge
A restricted metering device or plugged filter-drier
An overcharged system
A failed condenser fan

explanation: High superheat shows a starved evaporator, but normal subcooling shows plenty of condensed liquid available — the liquid is being held back. That combination points at a restriction, not a shortage; undercharge would show low subcooling too.

questionText: A walk-in freezer holds -5°F for two days after each service visit, then climbs to 20°F. Each visit the tech melts heavy evaporator frost. The correct fix is:

choices.text: Add refrigerant at each visit
Diagnose the defrost system (timer initiation, element heat, termination) instead of repeatedly clearing the symptom
Schedule weekly manual defrost visits
Lower the thermostat setpoint

explanation: Recurring frost blockage on a schedule means defrost is not doing its job. Melting the frost treats the symptom; verifying timer, elements, and termination stat cures the disease.

questionText: While repairing a reach-in you must open the sealed refrigerant circuit. Legally, the refrigerant must be:

choices.text: Vented outdoors away from people
Recovered into an approved cylinder by a certified technician
Released only if under 1 pound
Burned off with a torch

explanation: EPA Section 608 prohibits venting regulated refrigerants. Recovery into rated cylinders (never filled past 80%) by certified technicians is the legal requirement.

questionText: Three different refrigeration complaints in the same kitchen — a warm prep table, a struggling reach-in, and an ice machine at half production — most likely share:

choices.text: A refrigerant leak in a shared line
Grease-clogged condenser coils and hot, starved airflow at each unit
One failed compressor
A software fault

explanation: These are self-contained units; they share only their environment. Grease-dust on condensers and hot cramped placement is the kitchen-wide epidemic that produces simultaneous 'unrelated' refrigeration complaints.

questionText: An ice machine's cubes have become progressively shallower over a month, and harvest cycles run long. The water filter was last changed 14 months ago. The most likely root cause is:

choices.text: Refrigerant loss
Scale accumulation from exhausted water treatment affecting the evaporator, probes, and water flow
A worn compressor
Low ambient temperature

explanation: Progressive ice-quality decline plus long harvests plus overdue treatment is the scale signature: it thins water flow, blinds thickness sensing, and makes ice stick through harvest. Descale and restore treatment.

questionText: You arrive to a strong gas smell in a closed kitchen. Staff are still prepping. Your immediate sequence is:

choices.text: Turn on the exhaust hood to clear the gas, then investigate
Evacuate people without switching anything electrical, shut gas at the safest point, ventilate with doors, call the utility from outside
Open your combustible gas detector and begin leak-hunting at the ranges
Flip the lights on to see better, then search

explanation: In a gas atmosphere, any electrical switching — including the hood fan and lights — is an ignition source. People out, gas off, passive ventilation, call from outside. Diagnosis comes after the atmosphere is safe.

questionText: A gas convection oven ignites reliably but drops the flame after 8-10 seconds, three times, then locks out. Manifold pressure is correct and the flame looks healthy. The highest-probability fix is:

choices.text: Replace the gas valve
Clean the flame sensor rod and verify the burner ground path
Increase the manifold pressure slightly
Replace the ignition control board

explanation: Lights-then-drops with good flame is the flame-rectification signature: the microamp proving signal is too weak. An oxidized rod or corroded ground path chokes the signal; cleaning the rod and securing ground fixes most of these calls.

questionText: A café reports staff headaches every busy weekend. You find the make-up air unit failed months ago and the water heater flue shows backdraft staining. The mechanism endangering staff is:

choices.text: Refrigerant leaking from the walk-in
The negative kitchen pulling combustion products back down flues while burners run oxygen-starved — carbon monoxide accumulation
Detergent fumes from the dish machine
Ozone from the ice machine

explanation: Exhaust without make-up air drives the space negative: flues reverse and burners starve, both producing and trapping CO indoors — worst during busy periods when everything fires at once. This is an emergency-priority correction.

questionText: While servicing a charbroiler you notice its suppression nozzle has no cap and the system inspection tag expired five months ago. You should:

choices.text: Cap the nozzle with foil and update the tag
Document both conditions and notify the operator in writing that licensed fire-protection service is required
Remove the nozzle for cleaning
Ignore it — fire systems are not your trade

explanation: Suppression work belongs to licensed fire protection contractors, but a missing nozzle cap (grease plugs the orifice) and lapsed inspection are hazards you are professionally obligated to document and report — the middle path between tampering and ignoring.

questionText: Every gas appliance on a cook line went dead simultaneously at 6am. Breakers are on and the gas meter valve is open. Before condemning appliances, check:

choices.text: Each appliance's thermocouple
The exhaust fan and its proving interlock — cooking equipment cannot run without exhaust per NFPA 96
The water heater
Each appliance's regulator

explanation: Simultaneous line-wide shutdown points at a shared upstream cause. The hood interlock (fan failure or a dead proving switch) disables the entire line by design — the classic trap that eats hours if unknown.

questionText: A health inspector cited a restaurant's high-temp dish machine. The gauge shows 182°F rinse but a thermolabel run through a cycle does not turn. The discrepancy means:

choices.text: The thermolabel is defective — the gauge is proof enough
Water in the pipe is hot but the ware surface is not reaching 160°F — pressure, contact time, or gauge accuracy needs diagnosis
The inspector used the wrong test
The machine needs more detergent

explanation: Codes measure sanitizing at the dish surface, not in the plumbing. A hot gauge with a cold label points to weak rinse pressure/volume, short contact, or a lying gauge — the label is the legal truth-teller.

questionText: A dish machine's final rinse reads 150°F. Water entering the booster measures 112°F. A properly working booster on this supply would deliver approximately:

choices.text: 180°F — boosters always reach spec
About 152°F — a booster adds roughly 40°F to whatever it receives
140°F regardless of input

explanation: Boosters are sized for ~40°F rise: 112 + 40 ≈ 152°F, which matches the reading. The machine's booster is healthy; the building hot water supply is the fault to fix.

questionText: Entering a basement syrup room, you feel short of breath and lightheaded within seconds. The room houses a bulk CO2 system. You should:

choices.text: Prop the door and work quickly
Leave immediately, ventilate the space, and do not re-enter until the atmosphere is verified safe
Find the leak first, then ventilate
Put on a dust mask and continue

explanation: CO2 displaces air silently and kills in enclosed spaces; breathlessness IS the alarm. Exit first — no diagnosis, no heroics — ventilate, and re-enter only when the atmosphere is verified. A dust mask does nothing about displaced oxygen.

questionText: A blast chiller displays cabinet temperature 15°F warmer than your verified reference thermometer reads. The control cycles based on its own reading. The correct repair is:

choices.text: Offset the setpoint 15°F colder and leave it
Verify the probe's resistance against its chart, inspect its connector, and replace the drifted probe
Replace the control board
Recalibrate your reference thermometer to match the display

explanation: A drifted sensor makes a healthy board misbehave. Verify against the resistance-temperature chart (checking the connector too) and replace the lying input. Setpoint offsets mask food-safety-critical errors and drift further.

questionText: This is the third control board you have replaced in the same combi oven in a year. Before installing the fourth, the professional obligation is to:

choices.text: Order two boards to save a trip next time
Find what is killing the boards — moisture intrusion, supply voltage, grounding, compartment heat — and fix that
Recommend a different brand of oven
Install the board and add a surge strip

explanation: Boards are victims, not causes. Serial board deaths have an environmental killer — pressure-washing, brownouts, bad ground, blocked cooling — and replacement number four dies on schedule unless it is found.

questionText: A refrigerated prep rail measures 47°F product temperature during your PM visit. The correct professional action is:

choices.text: Note it and move on — 47°F is close enough
Inform the operator immediately (food safety threshold exceeded), diagnose, and document found and corrected temperatures
Quietly lower the setpoint and leave
Tell them to sell the product faster

explanation: Cold holding above ~40-41°F is a food-safety violation with real health consequences. The operator must know now (their product decision), and your documentation of found/corrected temps is part of the repair.

questionText: A reach-in compressor: C-to-R reads 2Ω, C-to-S reads 6Ω, R-to-S reads 8Ω, and all terminals read OL to the shell. The start capacitor measures 3µF against a 88-108µF rating. The diagnosis is:

choices.text: Grounded compressor — replace it
Open windings — replace the compressor
Windings healthy (2+6=8, isolated from shell); the failed start capacitor is the fault
Normal capacitor, bad relay

explanation: R-S equals C-R plus C-S and nothing is shorted to the case: the motor checks out. A start capacitor at 3µF versus an 88-108µF rating is dead — the cheap part explains the no-start, and the compressor lives.

questionText: A range moved from a propane food truck into a natural gas restaurant, connected without conversion, will exhibit:

choices.text: Severe over-firing and sooting
Weak, starved flames — propane orifices are too small for lower-pressure, lower-energy natural gas
Normal operation
Automatic compensation by the regulator

explanation: Propane orifices are sized for higher pressure and energy density. On natural gas they pass too little fuel: weak flames and useless burners. (The reverse mistake — NG orifices on propane — over-fires dangerously.) Conversion is a formal orifice/regulator procedure.

questionText: Commissioning a new dish machine, you find its drain hard-piped directly into the sewer line with no air gap. You should:

choices.text: Start it up — drains are the plumber's problem forever
Document the code deficiency, assign it to the plumbing scope in writing, and note the contamination risk it creates
Cut the pipe and create a gap yourself
Refuse to ever commission the machine

explanation: Food equipment drains require an air gap against sewage backflow into the machine. Deficiency handling: found, documented, routed to the owning trade in writing, with the risk stated — professional and drama-free.

questionText: An 11-year-old walk-in condensing unit (typical life 12-15 years) needs its second compressor in three years: $2,100 repair vs $5,800 replacement. The professional framing to the customer is:

choices.text: Just authorize the repair — it is cheaper today
Present the numbers, age, and failure history honestly — including why the last compressor died — and let the customer decide
Insist on replacement
Repair it without discussing options

explanation: Repair-versus-replace is the customer's economic decision, made well only with honest framing: cost ratio, remaining life, the repeat-failure pattern (and its root cause), and downtime risk. The tech supplies facts, not pressure.

questionText: A countertop steamer at a hard-water site burns out elements every 10-12 weeks. The repair that actually ends the pattern is:

choices.text: Heavier-duty elements
Installing/renewing water treatment and establishing a descaling schedule
A spare set of elements left on site
Lowering the thermostat

explanation: Scale blankets make elements overheat and die on a schedule set by the water. Treatment plus descaling is the root-cause repair; every other option just re-arms the same failure.
referenceBookSection: Module 12 — Electric Cooking; Module 24 — PM Programs

questionText: After repairing a leak and evacuating a self-contained prep table to 350 microns (holding), the correct charging procedure is:

choices.text: Add refrigerant until the box gets cold
Weigh in the exact data-plate charge with a scale, then verify with superheat/subcooling
Charge until suction pressure reads 20 psi
Fill until the sight glass clears

explanation: Self-contained equipment publishes its factory charge. The scale removes guesswork; superheat and subcooling confirm the system behaves correctly with that charge.

questionText: Glassware exits a low-temp machine with a persistent haze. A drop of vinegar on the haze does nothing. The haze is:

choices.text: Mineral scale — descale the machine
Detergent residue — check for over-dosing and weak final rinse
Chlorine bleaching
Etched glass from hard water

explanation: The vinegar test separates films: acid dissolves mineral haze instantly; detergent residue ignores it. Persistent haze means chemistry — dosing rate and rinse effectiveness — not scale.

questionText: A hood exhaust fan motor on the roof hums and trips its overload. The fan blades are caked solid with grease. Before electrical testing, consider that:

choices.text: The motor windings must have failed
Grease-bound bearings and blade imbalance can mechanically lock or overload a healthy motor — check rotation by hand (locked out) first
The capacitor is always the cause on roofs
The overload is undersized

explanation: A mechanically bound motor hums and trips exactly like an electrically failed one. With power locked out, spin the shaft by hand: seized bearings and caked blades are the roof-top reality check before any parts are ordered.

questionText: Several appliances' electronic controls reboot near-simultaneously during the dinner rush most nights. The measurement that will likely explain it is:

choices.text: Resistance of each control board's power input
Supply voltage at the panel under full rush load, compared to nameplate requirements
Microamps at each flame sensor
Ground rod resistance

explanation: Synchronized multi-appliance resets track shared supply, not individual boards. Voltage measured under peak load (when everything fires) reveals the brownout that idle-time measurements miss.

questionText: A hood that captured smoke perfectly for years now spills at the front edge. The filters are clean and the exhaust fan runs. What changed is most likely:

choices.text: The hood shrank
Building airflow: failed make-up air, a new door/HVAC arrangement, or cross-drafts disrupting capture
The cooking got smokier
The duct grew longer

explanation: Capture is an air-balance outcome. With clean filters and a running fan, hunting what changed in the room — make-up air health, new drafts, propped doors, HVAC changes — finds the spill cause.

questionText: Ice is legally classified as:

choices.text: A beverage additive
Food — making the ice machine a food-contact surface subject to health code
Water, unregulated once frozen
Equipment output, not inspected

explanation: Health codes treat ice as food and the machine as a food-contact surface: scheduled cleaning AND sanitizing, documented, is compliance — the reason slime in an ice machine is a citation, not a cosmetic issue.

questionText: A grocery's open multi-deck case fails temperature checks only on its top two shelves, where product is stacked well above the load-line stickers. The physics of the failure is:

choices.text: Heat rises inside the case
Product above the load line sits outside the air curtain and blocks it for shelves below
The top shelves are farthest from the compressor
LED lights overheat the top zone

explanation: The air curtain flows along the load-line boundary. Over-stacked product both sits outside the refrigerated envelope and tears the curtain that protects everything beneath it — an operations fix, not a refrigeration fix.

questionText: A soft-serve machine's gearbox was replaced after seizing. Two months later the new gearbox is grinding. The prior tech never checked:

choices.text: The mix expiration dates
Why the first gearbox seized — worn beater bearings/auger misalignment or product leaking past a failed shaft seal into the gearbox
The compressor charge
The condenser coil

explanation: Gearboxes rarely die alone: product past a failed shaft seal, or side-loads from worn beater support, killed the first one and is killing the second. Root cause or repeat — the course's oldest rule.

questionText: A customer demands you declare their failed compressor 'a warranty job' on the spot. The correct response is to:

choices.text: Agree to preserve the relationship
Verify model, serial, install date, and warranty terms before committing to anything
Refuse and bill full rate
Split the cost fifty-fifty

explanation: Warranty is a verifiable fact, not a negotiation or a favor. Premature promises become someone's unfunded cost; verification takes minutes and protects everyone.

questionText: On a millivolt fryer, the thermopile reads 620mV open-circuit but drops to 90mV when the main valve should open, and the burner never fires. This indicates:

choices.text: A healthy system warming up
A weak thermopile that cannot hold voltage under load — replace it
Excess gas pressure
A miswired thermostat

explanation: Open-circuit voltage means little; the test is voltage under load. Collapsing from 620mV to 90mV when the valve coil draws current is the signature of a thermopile that can no longer deliver — replace it.
referenceBookSection: Module 19 — Ranges, Combi Ovens & Specialty Equipment

questionText: A combi oven at a new location fails with steam faults within weeks, repeatedly. The site connected it to untreated well water 'temporarily.' The professional position is:

choices.text: Keep repairing under the service agreement
Document that operation without the manufacturer-specified water treatment is causing the failures (and likely voiding warranty), and require treatment as part of the fix
Install a garden-hose filter
Recommend a different oven brand

explanation: Combi manufacturers specify water quality because scale destroys generators, probes, and valves on a schedule. Repairs without treatment are rented failures; documenting the requirement protects the customer, the warranty, and your agreement.
referenceBookSection: Module 19 — Combi Ovens; Module 24 — PM Programs

questionText: A dish machine trips its GFCI intermittently, always during the wash cycle. The most productive suspect list is:

choices.text: The GFCI is oversensitive — replace it with a standard breaker
Moisture or element/wiring leakage to ground that appears when the wash pump and tank heater run — megger/leakage-test the loads
Low water pressure
Detergent conductivity

explanation: A GFCI trips on real leakage current. Cycle-correlated trips point at loads energized in that cycle — wash pump motor, tank element — leaking to ground when hot and wet. Removing GFCI protection in a wet environment is never the fix.

questionText: After replacing a walk-in's condenser fan motor, the repair is verified by:

choices.text: The fan spinning when powered
Watching the system pull the box down to setpoint and confirming head pressure returned to normal range
The customer signing the invoice
No error lights on the panel

explanation: The fix is verified under real conditions doing its real job: temperature recovery and normal operating pressures — not merely a spinning part. 'It runs' and 'it works' are different claims.

questionText: A newly hired kitchen tech may legally do all of the following EXCEPT:

choices.text: Replace a fryer thermostat
Open a reach-in's sealed refrigerant system without EPA 608 certification
Clean condenser coils
Test a heating element with an ohmmeter

explanation: EPA Section 608 certification is federally required before opening refrigerant circuits or purchasing regulated refrigerants. Electrical and mechanical work has no equivalent federal gate — 608 is the legal line.
referenceBookSection: Module 27 — Career in Commercial Kitchen Field Service
